title: Paper Explained: FedSIR: Spectral Client Identification and Relabeling for Federated Learning with Noisy Labels - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle:
- Federated learning: fix wrong labels without sharing raw data
- Cleaning noisy labels across devices in federated learning
- Identify clean clients, relabel noisy data in federated learning

content.background:
Federated learning (FL) lets many devices or organizations collaborate to train a shared model without moving their raw data around. This is great for privacy, but it also means the labels (the answers) coming from different places are not all trustworthy. In the real world, people tag images, categorize sounds, or annotate data imperfectly. When you combine updates from many clients, these labeling mistakes can pile up and mislead the whole model, especially because different clients have their own unique mistakes.

Most existing fixes try to fix the problem either by designing loss functions that are supposed to be tolerant of mislabeled data or by watching how the training behaves to decide which labels to trust. But FL adds extra hurdles: each client may have very different kinds of data (non-iid), labeling quality varies widely, and you can’t spend lots of time or bandwidth checking every label on every device. This makes traditional noise-robust tricks less effective or much harder to apply in practice.

That gap—how to deal with noisy labels when data is spread across many clients and privacy rules limit data sharing—creates a real need for new ideas. The motivation behind this research is to find a way to identify which clients provide cleaner signals and which contribute noisy labels, using information that doesn’t require heavy communication. If we can better handle label noise in this setting, FL could be trusted and deployed in more real-world scenarios, like healthcare, mobile apps, or any domain where data quality varies a lot across contributors.
content.methodology:
FedSIR tackles federated learning with noisy labels by looking at the problem from a new angle: the geometry of how features for each class sit in space, rather than just tweaking loss functions. The idea is to first separate the “good” (clean) clients from the ones with mislabeled data, then let the clean clients guide the noisy ones without sharing raw data, and finally train in a way that is robust to any remaining label noise. Think of it as a collaborative choir where some singers may be misreading the lyrics, and a few trusted singers help identify and correct the mistakes without exchanging private recordings.

- Step 1: Identify clean vs. noisy clients using spectral consistency. Imagine each class as a musical note that appears as a direction in feature space. Across many clients, the clean ones tend to have similar, stable directions for each class, forming coherent subspaces. Noisy clients, whose labels are corrupted, disrupt this spectral harmony. FedSIR analyzes the geometry of these class-wise feature subspaces (the spectral structure) with minimal communication to decide which clients are clean and which are noisy.
- What this buys you: you can separate the participants without sending raw data, and you end up with a set of reliable clients that can act as anchors for the rest.

- Step 2: Clean clients provide spectral references and noisy clients relabel. Clean clients don’t reveal data; instead, they share spectral references—essentially guidance about the typical directions and subspaces that correspond to each class. Noisy clients then use these references to relabel potentially corrupted samples. They do this by (a) looking at the dominant class directions (the main melody) and (b) leveraging residual subspaces (the finer harmonies) to decide a better label for each uncertain sample.
- What this buys you: noisy labels are corrected in a privacy-preserving way, using the agreed-upon spectral structure as a guide rather than guessing blindly.

- Step 3: Noise-aware training to stabilize learning. After relabeling, FedSIR trains with a three-pronged strategy: (1) a logit-adjusted loss that reduces the negative impact of remaining label noise, (2) knowledge distillation where the trusted (clean) clients’ models guide the noisy ones, and (3) distance-aware aggregation that weighs updates by how confidently or closely they align with the spectral references. In short, the model learns not only from the relabeled data but also from a calibrated mix of guidance and weighted contributions, which helps prevent noisy information from derailing training.
- What this buys you: a more robust federated optimization process that stays stable and performs well even when some clients still have noisy labels.

Overall, FedSIR combines a spectral (geometry-based) way to identify clean participants, a relabeling mechanism guided by clean clients’ spectral references, and a noise-aware training loop that blends adjusted losses, teacher–student guidance, and careful aggregation. The result is better performance in federated learning with noisy labels, achieved with low communication overhead and without sharing raw data. If you want to see the approach in action, the authors provide the code link in the paper.
content.results:
FedSIR tackles a common problem in federated learning: when different clients (devices or organizations) have mislabeled data, the global model learns from bad labels and its performance falls. The big idea is to look at the shape of the data in a shared, lightweight way—its spectral structure in feature space—and use that to separate clean clients from noisy ones. This lets the method identify which clients are likely providing reliable information without needing lots of extra communication or centralized data.

The approach works in three steps. First, FedSIR examines how class-specific features line up across clients to judge consistency, labeling some clients as clean and others as noisy based on this spectral check. Next, the clean clients quietly share spectral references (not raw data) that help the noisy clients relabel their questionable samples. They do this using two ideas: dominant class directions (the main cues that separate classes) and residual subspaces (the trickier, fine-grained directions). Finally, the training process itself becomes more robust: it combines a noise-aware loss, knowledge distillation from the cleaner references, and a carefully weighted aggregation that pays more attention to closer, more trustworthy contributions. All of this is designed to work with minimal extra communication.

Empirical results show FedSIR consistently beating the current best methods for federated learning with noisy labels on standard benchmarks. In short, it not only identifies which clients are reliable, but also uses their reliable information to clean and guide the others, leading to a steadier and better-performing global model. This is practically important: it makes federated learning more robust in real-world settings where labeling noise is common (think crowdsourced labels or distributed medical data) and reduces the need for heavy loss-tuning or data centralization. The work also provides a publicly available implementation, making it easier for researchers and practitioners to try and build on this idea.
content.significance:
FedSIR matters today because in the real world, data used to train AI is not only vast and distributed, but also noisy. In federated learning, many devices or organizations contribute models without sharing their raw data, which raises the risk that mislabeled or low-quality data from some clients drags down the whole system. FedSIR tackles this head-on with a three-part strategy that uses the geometry of feature representations (the “spectral” structure) to tell apart clean clients from those with label problems. Then it lets clean clients help relabel questionable data on noisy clients and finally trains with a noise-aware approach that combines several robustness ideas. All of this is designed to work with minimal extra communication, which is crucial in large, real-world deployments.

Looking to the long term, FedSIR points to a broader shift in how we build robust, privacy-preserving AI systems. Rather than relying only on clever loss functions or heavy training tricks, it introduces a practical, spectrum-based way to diagnose data quality across many participants and to improve labels collaboratively. This idea of using the global geometry of feature spaces to identify and correct data problems could inspire a family of methods for robust federated learning—things like client clustering, adaptive relabeling, and personalized, noise-aware training. As AI systems become more distributed (for example, on-device fine-tuning or privacy-preserving collaboration across hospitals or banks), such spectral and multi-stage techniques could become standard tools to keep models accurate even when data quality varies a lot across participants.

In terms of real-world impact, you’ll start seeing principles from FedSIR in applications that must protect privacy while learning from messy data. Think healthcare imaging networks that span multiple hospitals without sharing patient data, or mobile/edge AI systems where user-generated data is labeled imperfectly or inconsistently. Even large, modern AI pipelines (like those behind ChatGPT-style models) grapple with data quality at scale; ideas from FedSIR about identifying trustworthy data sources, relabeling thoughtfully, and using diffusion of knowledge (distillation) across clients can influence how we design robust, privacy-preserving training in the future. The paper’s open-code release also helps researchers and practitioners test, extend, and adopt these ideas, helping the field move toward reliable federated learning in the messy, real world.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Spectral Subspace Analysis: The Heart of FedSIR
concept_explanation.content:
Think of this idea like listening to a choir and trying to tell who is singing in tune. In FedSIR, each class (for example, cats, dogs, and birds) has a characteristic “tone” or direction in the model’s internal feature space. If a client’s data are clean, the features for each class cluster around a clear set of directions. If a client has label noise, its features get spread in odd ways and don’t line up with the others. By listening to how these class directions (subspaces) look across clients, we can separate reliable clients from the unreliable ones, and we can clean up the noisy labels using the reliable ones as a reference.

Here is the idea step by step. First, for every client, the model extracts feature representations for its local data, and for each class it builds a covariance-like picture of how those features vary. From this, it finds the dominant directions of variation—these are the principal directions of a class, i.e., the top eigenvectors that span a subspace for that class. Second, it checks how similar these class subspaces are across different clients. If a client’s class directions align well with the consensus (the majority of clients), that client is considered clean. If a client’s subspaces look misaligned or chaotic, that client is flagged as likely noisy. A simple way to measure this alignment is to compare the angles between subspaces or how much information is preserved when projecting one client’s features onto another’s subspace.

Once clean and noisy clients are identified, FedSIR uses the clean clients to provide spectral references. For each class, clean clients supply its dominant directions (the main subspace) and the residual subspace (the directions that explain remaining variation). Noisy clients then relabel their potentially corrupted samples by looking at how well a sample’s feature vector projects onto these references. In practice, you project a sample onto the dominant class subspace and onto its residual subspace, and you choose the class whose subspace best reconstructs the sample. If a sample sits well in the reference subspace for “cat” and poorly for “dog” or “bird,” it’s relabeled accordingly. The residual subspace helps catch tricky cases where a sample doesn’t fit neatly into a single dominant class direction, giving a more nuanced relabeling.

After relabeling, the training process itself becomes more robust. FedSIR combines several ideas to handle remaining label noise: a logit-adjusted loss that accounts for class imbalance and uncertainty; knowledge distillation where clean clients’ predictions guide the learning of others (soft targets help prevent overfitting to noisy labels); and distance-aware aggregation that weighs updates by how far samples are from their class subspaces (reducing influence from suspicious examples). Put together, this means the model learns with less influence from mislabeled data while still benefiting from the diverse, privacy-preserving data spread across clients.

Why is this important, and where could you use it? In real-world federated learning, you often train across many devices or institutions where labels aren’t perfect—people mistag photos, clinics mislabel records, or devices collect noisy sensory data. Traditional FL methods that just tweak loss functions can falter when label noise is widespread. Spectral Subspace Analysis adds a different, geometry-based lens: it looks at the shape of how features separate by class, not just the loss value. This makes the system more robust to mislabeling and can improve accuracy without shouting back at the network with heavier data exchange. Practical applications include healthcare collaborations with noisy diagnostics, mobile or edge-device learning where users label data imperfectly, and cross-institution studies where labeling standards differ. If you want to experiment further, the authors provide code in FedSIR’s repository, which implements the spectral identification, relabeling, and noise-aware training pipeline.

summary: This paper introduces FedSIR, a multi-stage method that uses the spectrum of client feature representations to identify clean and noisy clients and relabel data, improving the robustness of federated learning with noisy labels.
